title: Statistical design offers a better way to plan complex experiments
gist: A sponsored/native-ad piece for JMP Statistical Discovery promotes Design of Experiments (DOE) methodology -- illustrated via a Johnson Matthey biocatalysis scale-up case -- as a systematic replacement for one-factor-at-a-time trial-and-error experimentation in chemical and life-science R&D, framing it as increasingly integrated with Bayesian optimization and machine learning.

themes.theme: The core scale-up problem DOE addresses

insights.claim: In enzyme-driven bioprocess scale-up, yields that are robust at lab scale become highly sensitive to mixing, heat and mass transfer, and feeding strategy at larger reactor scale, increasing waste and energy use under certain conditions even when the underlying chemistry is well characterized.
insights.speaker: Nature Biotechnology
insights.so_what: Scale-up failure driven by parameter interactions (not the core chemistry) is a recurring, costly bottleneck in biomanufacturing capex planning; process-design tooling that reduces scale-up risk directly affects time-to-market and cost-per-batch for biologics and fine-chemical manufacturers.
insights.quote: Yields that are robust in small-scale experiments become highly sensitive to mixing, heat and mass transfer and feeding strategies, increasing waste and energy use under certain conditions.

insights.claim: Traditional trial-and-error scale-up work adjusts one variable at a time, which the JMP expert quoted compares to 'cutting holes in a door and trying to see into a room' -- an approach that fails to reveal how variables interact.
insights.speaker: Pilar Gómez-Jiménez
insights.so_what: A crisp mental model (the 'door' analogy) for why one-factor-at-a-time experimentation systematically misses interaction effects -- useful shorthand for evaluating any R&D organization's process-development maturity and whether it still relies on outdated single-variable optimization.
insights.quote: That’s like cutting holes in a door and trying to see into a room... DOE lets you make the holes in the right places, so your view is as if the door was open. You can see clearly how factors interact and make decisions based on the data.

themes.theme: DOE as a business/decision-making tool, not just a lab technique

insights.claim: DOE-derived predictive models can extend beyond technical performance metrics to estimate cost and environmental impact, allowing R&D teams to compare different process options early in a project when changes are cheapest to make.
insights.speaker: Pilar Gómez-Jiménez
insights.so_what: Positions DOE as a capital-allocation and ESG-cost-forecasting tool embedded inside R&D, not merely a lab efficiency technique -- relevant for process chemistry/biomanufacturing companies evaluating where to front-load statistical rigor to cut late-stage scale-up costs.
insights.quote: Such models can extend beyond technical performance to include estimates of cost and environmental impact, helping teams compare different options early on.

insights.claim: Applying DOE can reduce the total amount of experimental work required later in a development project, according to over a decade of hands-on application by a senior principal scientist at Johnson Matthey.
insights.speaker: Pilar Gómez-Jiménez
insights.so_what: A direct efficiency claim (less downstream experimentation) that translates into faster time-to-market and lower R&D spend for chemical/pharma process development teams adopting statistical experimental design at scale.
insights.quote: In practice, Gómez‑Jiménez says, the method can reduce the amount of experimental work required later in a project.

themes.theme: Convergence of DOE with modern data science and AI tooling

insights.claim: JMP's software combines data handling, visualization, and machine-learning tools with modern experimental design methods such as Bayesian optimization into what is described as a 'complete data science toolbox' for scientists and engineers.
insights.speaker: Phil Kay
insights.so_what: Shows the classical statistics discipline of Design of Experiments is being actively merged with Bayesian optimization and ML inside commercial lab-software platforms -- a relevant trend for tracking how AI-native optimization methods are being embedded into legacy scientific software rather than replacing it outright.
insights.quote: JMP offers a complete data science toolbox for scientists and engineers, combining data handling, visualization and machine-learning tools with modern experimental design methods such as Bayesian optimization

insights.claim: Visualization features built into DOE software let researchers present results as 'immediate, understandable graphics' instead of raw p-values and model parameters, which is framed as increasing the ability to be 'impactful and compelling' when communicating results.
insights.speaker: Phil Kay
insights.so_what: Highlights a communication/adoption barrier for statistical methods in R&D organizations -- vendors are explicitly selling visualization as a way to get non-statistician stakeholders (management, cross-functional teams) to act on DOE results faster.
insights.quote: It means you can be impactful and compelling, with immediate, understandable graphics, rather than p‑values and model parameters

insights.claim: The broader shift described is toward 'democratization' of statistical tools, enabling researchers without formal statistics training to apply DOE methods directly to their own subject-matter questions.
insights.speaker: Pilar Gómez-Jiménez
insights.so_what: Software-driven democratization of statistical methods lowers the skill barrier for adopting rigorous experimental design across chemical and life-science labs, which could broaden the addressable market for statistical software vendors like JMP beyond dedicated statisticians to bench scientists and engineers directly.
insights.quote: I am a strong believer in the democratization of tools. This kind of software allows researchers to apply statistics to their own questions and their own fields. It’s the synergy between statistics and subject‑matter expertise.
